prompt: Using ONLY the provided subscription agreement, answer: The customer is dissatisfied with the service (though the Provider is not in breach of the agreement) and, on 1 May 2026, four months into the initial term, wants to stop paying and leave. (1) Can the customer terminate the agreement now and obtain a refund of the remaining prepaid fees? (2) What is the earliest date this agreement can actually end, and precisely what must the customer do, and by when, to make that happen? Cite the exact clause numbers you rely on. If the document does not determine something, say so explicitly rather than guessing.

inputs.name: subscription-agreement.md
inputs.content:
# SaaS Subscription Agreement

Clause 1. Initial Term. The Initial Term is 12 months, beginning on the Effective Date of 1 January 2026 and ending on 31 December 2026.

Clause 2. Auto-Renewal. On expiry of the Initial Term the agreement automatically renews for successive 12-month renewal terms, unless either party gives the other written notice of non-renewal at least 60 days before the end of the then-current term.

Clause 3. No Termination for Convenience. Neither party may terminate this agreement for convenience during any term. There is no mid-term exit for dissatisfaction or any other reason not expressly stated in this agreement.

Clause 4. Termination for Cause. Either party may terminate this agreement for cause if the other party materially breaches the agreement and fails to cure that breach within 30 days after receiving written notice describing the breach.

Clause 5. Fees. The annual fee is due in advance within 15 days of the start of each term. Prepaid fees are non-refundable except as expressly provided in Clause 6.

Clause 6. Refund on Provider Breach. If the Customer terminates for cause under Clause 4 because the Provider materially breached, the Provider shall refund the prorated portion of prepaid fees covering the remainder of the terminated term. If the Provider terminates for cause because the Customer breached, no refund is due.

Clause 7. Data Export on Termination. Upon any termination or expiry, the Customer may export its data through the self-service API during a 30-day window following the termination or expiry date. After that 30-day window, the Provider permanently deletes the Customer's data. The Provider is not obligated to perform any manual data extraction.

Clause 8. Notices. All notices under this agreement must be in writing. An email sent to the other party's designated account administrator satisfies the writing requirement.

grading.guidance: Correct answer to (1): NO. Clause 3 expressly bars termination for convenience mid-term, and dissatisfaction is not a ground for exit. The Provider is not in material breach, so the customer has no termination-for-cause right under Clause 4. Because there is no valid termination, no refund arises; and even on termination Clause 5 makes prepaid fees non-refundable except under Clause 6, which grants a refund only when the Customer terminates for cause due to Provider breach - not the case here. So the customer cannot leave on 1 May 2026 and gets no refund. Correct answer to (2): The earliest the agreement can end is 31 December 2026, the end of the 12-month Initial Term (Clause 1). To make it actually end then (rather than auto-renew), the customer must, per Clause 2, give the Provider written notice of non-renewal at least 60 days before 31 December 2026, i.e., on or before 1 November 2026; per Clause 8 an email to the Provider's designated account administrator satisfies the written-notice requirement. After expiry the customer has a 30-day self-service API window to export data before deletion (Clause 7). THE TRAP: reading Clause 4's '30 days to cure' as a general 30-day termination-for-convenience notice period and concluding the customer can give 30 days' notice and leave around 1 June 2026 (with or without a prorated refund). Clause 4's 30 days is a cure period available only after a material breach, and requires a breach by the Provider that does not exist here. Penalize heavily any answer that lets the customer exit mid-term, grants a refund, treats dissatisfaction as cause, or uses the 30-day cure period as a convenience-exit notice. A 10 says NO exit and NO refund citing Clauses 3, 4, 5, 6, and gives the earliest end date as 31 December 2026 with the non-renewal notice due by 1 November 2026 citing Clauses 1, 2, 8. A 1 says the customer can terminate on 30 days' notice and/or get a prorated refund.

calibrationAnchors.answer: Yes, the customer can leave. Under Clause 4 they just give 30 days' written notice, so the agreement ends around 1 June 2026, and under Clause 6 they get a prorated refund of the prepaid fees for the rest of the year.

(1) No. Clause 3 bars termination for convenience during any term, and mere dissatisfaction is not a permitted ground. Clause 4 termination for cause is unavailable because the Provider is not in material breach - and its 30-day period is a cure window after a breach, not a convenience-notice period. With no valid termination there is no refund; moreover Clause 5 makes prepaid fees non-refundable except under Clause 6, which applies only when the Customer terminates for cause due to a Provider breach, which is not the case. So the customer cannot exit on 1 May 2026 and receives no refund. (2) The earliest the agreement can end is 31 December 2026, the end of the 12-month Initial Term (Clause 1). To prevent auto-renewal the customer must, under Clause 2, give written notice of non-renewal at least 60 days before 31 December 2026 - i.e., on or before 1 November 2026 - and under Clause 8 an email to the Provider's designated account administrator counts as written notice. After expiry the customer has a 30-day self-service API window to export data before deletion (Clause 7).
